mcqs.explanation: The size of a union is equal to the size of its largest member. Since int and float are both 4 bytes (the largest), the union size is 4 bytes. All members share this same memory location.

When we assign 65 to t.x (which is binary 01000001), the same memory location is interpreted as char t.y. The ASCII value 65 corresponds to character 'A', so both t.x and t.y display their respective interpretations of the same memory.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is TRUE about unions in C?

options.text: All members can store values simultaneously
Union allocates memory equal to sum of all members
All members share the same memory location
Union cannot be used inside structures
Union members must all be of the same type

mcqs.explanation: In a union, all members share the same memory location. Only one member should be used at a time because assigning a value to one member overwrites the others.

mcqs.question: How do you declare an anonymous union inside a structure?

options.text: struct Example { union { int x; char y; }; };
struct Example { anonymous union { int x; char y; }; };
union Example = { int x; char y; };
struct Example { union name { int x; char y; }; };

mcqs.explanation: An anonymous union is declared without a name inside a structure. Its members can be accessed directly as if they were structure members, making the syntax cleaner.

mcqs.question: What is the correct way to initialize only the char member of a union?
mcqs.explanation: In C, designated initializers using .member_name syntax allow initializing specific union members. The syntax .c = 'A' specifically initializes the char member.

options.text: Compilation error - anonymous union cannot be used like this

mcqs.explanation: This is an anonymous union inside the structure. Members of anonymous unions can be accessed directly as if they were members of the structure. Since int 65 is ASCII 'A', s.y will display 'A'.

mcqs.question: Which scenario is most appropriate for using a union?

options.text: When you need to store multiple values simultaneously
When memory efficiency is critical and only one value is needed at a time
When you need to sort different data types
When you want to create an array of different types
When you need permanent storage of all members

mcqs.explanation: Unions are ideal when you need to store different data types in the same memory location but only one will be used at a time, saving memory compared to structures.

mcqs.question: What is a tagged union?

options.text: A union with a name tag
A union combined with an enum indicating the active member
A union inside a structure with a tag
A union of tagged (labeled) data types
A union that can only store character data

mcqs.explanation: A tagged union (also called discriminated union) combines a union with a separate field (usually an enum) that indicates which member is currently valid, providing type safety.

mcqs.question:
Consider: union Value { int i; float f; };
If we assign i=10 and then read f, what happens?

options.text: Both i and f are zero

mcqs.explanation: Reading a different member than the one last written to results in undefined behavior. The bit pattern of integer 10 is interpreted as a float, which produces a meaningless value, not 10.0.
The union size equals the largest member. Double is 8 bytes, which is the largest, so 8 bytes are allocated. Due to alignment requirements, it might be padded to 8 bytes.

mcqs.question: Which operator is used to access union members through a pointer?
mcqs.explanation: The arrow operator (->) is used to access union members through a pointer, just like with structures. For example: union_ptr->member_name.

mcqs.question: What is the primary advantage of union over structure?
mcqs.explanation: The primary advantage of unions is memory efficiency. Since all members share the same memory location, only the size of the largest member is allocated, unlike structures which allocate space for all members.

mcqs.question: Can a union contain a structure as a member?

options.text: No, unions can only contain primitive types
Yes, unions can contain any valid C data type including structures
Only if the structure is empty
Only in C++ not in C
Yes, but only if structure has no members

mcqs.explanation: Yes, unions can contain structures as members. This is useful for creating complex data types where different interpretations of the same memory are needed.
On a little-endian system, the least significant byte is stored at the lowest address. So bytes[0]=0x78 (LSB) and bytes[3]=0x12 (MSB). The output depends on system endianness.

mcqs.question: Which of the following correctly defines a union with members name (char[20]), age (int), and salary (float)?

options.text: union Employee { char name[20]; int age; float salary; };
union { char name[20]; int age; float salary; } Employee;
Both A and B are correct
Only A is correct as it has a name

mcqs.explanation: Both syntaxes are valid. A defines a union type named Employee, while B defines an anonymous union and creates a variable named Employee. Both declare a union with the specified members.

mcqs.question: What is the minimum guaranteed size of an empty union in C?

options.text: Implementation defined, but at least 1 byte

mcqs.explanation: In C, even an empty union must have at least 1 byte to have a unique address. However, this may vary by implementation.

mcqs.question: To safely use a union for storing different types, you should:

options.text: Always assign all members at once
Use a separate type indicator (enum/integer) to track which member is active
Never use unions with primitive types
Initialize unions to zero before use

mcqs.explanation: For safe union usage, maintain a separate indicator (tag field) that tracks which member was last written. This helps avoid reading the wrong interpretation of the shared memory.

mcqs.question: What happens when you try to access a union member that was never initialized?
mcqs.explanation: Accessing an uninitialized union member results in undefined behavior. The memory contains whatever garbage values were there before, unless the entire union was zero-initialized.
